claims.evidence_text: Wheat Pre-harvest Sprout (PHS) occurs when late-season rains cause grain to sprout before harvest, resulting in reduced grain quality and yield loss. Genetic factors determine susceptibility to PHS.
claims.claim_summary: Pre-harvest sprouting occurs when late-season rains cause mature wheat grain to break dormancy and sprout, reducing quality and yield; susceptibility is genetically determined.

claims.evidence_text: Harvesting grain as soon as it reaches physiological maturity is the best way to prevent PHS damage. The less time grain is exposed to potential late-season moisture, the lower the risk of PHS.
claims.claim_summary: Harvesting promptly at physiological maturity and selecting resistant cultivars minimizes pre-harvest sprouting risk.

claims.evidence_text: Flour prepared from PHS-damaged grain will have partially-degraded starch and gluten and lower falling numbers (a metric tied to bread quality where a lower score means poor bread quality).
claims.claim_summary: Pre-harvest sprouting lowers falling number (degraded starch/gluten), indicating poorer bread-making quality.

claims.evidence_text: Harvesting grain as soon as it reaches physiological maturity is the best way to prevent PHS damage.
claims.claim_summary: Physiological maturity is the target harvest timing point to minimize sprouting damage.
